Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 30 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== CURRENT STATE ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 20:
        boundary = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in boundary]
        cols = [p[1] for p in boundary]
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, rows[{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols[{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], children={n['children']}")

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nAgent area:")
for i in range(28, 36):
    print(f"Row {i}: {ascii_lines[i][18:38]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Very interesting! After the LEFT move, the right agent seems to have merged with the left agent's old position. Let me analyze:
- id=6: N color, 5 pixels, rows 30-31, cols 21-23 - this is the top part of the right agent (was at cols 24-26, now at cols 21-23)
- id=8: W color, 4 pixels, rows 31-32, cols 21-23 - this is the bottom part of the right agent
- id=11: N color, 8 pixels, rows 33-35, cols 21-23 - this is the left agent (still at cols 21-23!)
Wait, both agents are at cols 21-23? That can't be right. Let me look at the ASCII more carefully:
Row 30: wwwNNNwwwwwwwwwppppp
Row 31: wwwNWNwwwwwwwwwppppp
Row 32: wwwWWWwwwwwwwwwppppp
Row 33: wwwNNNgggggggggppppp
Row 34: wwwNcNgggggggggppppp
Row 35: wwwNNNgggggggggppppp
So at cols 21-23:
- Row 30: NNN (top of right agent)
- Row 31: NWN (middle of right agent with W center)
- Row 32: WWW (bottom of right agent... but this should be NNN)
- Row 33: NNN (top of left agent)
- Row 34: NcN (middle of left agent with c center)
- Row 35: NNN (bottom of left agent)
So the right agent is at rows 30-32, cols 21-23, and the left agent is at rows 33-35, cols 21-23. They're stacked vertically! The right agent moved from rows 30-32, cols 24-26 to rows 30-32, cols 21-23 (moved LEFT by 3 cells). The left agent is still at rows 33-35, cols 21-23.
Wait, but the left agent was at rows 33-35, cols 21-23 before. And the right agent was at rows 30-32. So they're in different row ranges. The right agent is above the left agent.
Looking at the segmentation:
- id=6: N, 5 pixels, rows 30-31, cols 21-23 - top 2 rows of right agent
- id=8: W, 4 pixels, rows 31-32, cols 21-23 - bottom part of right agent
- id=11: N, 8 pixels, rows 33-35, cols 21-23 - left agent (intact)
So the right agent is now at rows 30-32, cols 21-23. The bottom row of the right agent (row 32) is "WWW" instead of "NNN". This is because the right agent's center is W, and somehow the bottom row became white.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, id=6 has 5 pixels and id=8 has 4 pixels. The right agent was originally 8 pixels (3x3 minus 1 center = 8). Now it's split into 5 + 4 = 9 pixels. That's more than 8. Something weird is happening.
Actually, I think what happened is that the right agent moved LEFT and overlapped with the left agent's space. Since they're in different row ranges (right agent: rows 30-32, left agent: rows 33-35), they can coexist at the same columns.
But wait, the right agent was at rows 30-32, cols 24-26 before. Moving LEFT by 3 cells puts it at rows 30-32, cols 21-23. The left agent is at rows 33-35, cols 21-23. So they're stacked vertically, with the right agent above the left agent.
Now, the right agent is at cols 21-23, which is where the left c-frame entrance would be. The left c-frame is at cols 11-15, rows 32-36. So the right agent (rows 30-32) is above the left c-frame (rows 32-36).
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me think about what the goal might be.
I've been exploring mechanics for 30 actions. Let me try a completely different approach. What if I:
1. Click on the left agent to activate it
2. Move it LEFT into the left c-frame
The left agent is at rows 33-35, cols 21-23. The left c-frame interior is cols 12-14, rows 33-35. So I need to move LEFT by (21-12)/3 = 3 moves.
But the right agent is at rows 30-32, cols 21-23, which is above the left agent. This shouldn't block the left agent from moving LEFT.
Let me try clicking the left agent and then moving LEFT 3 times.
Actually, wait. The left agent's row range is 33-35, and the left c-frame's row range is 32-36. The c-frame interior (white square) is at rows 33-35, cols 12-14. So the left agent can move into the c-frame.
But I need to first activate the left agent. Let me click on it.
